summary: Dan Luu recounts his circuitous 15+ year journey into programming, starting with failed childhood attempts, studying electrical engineering and math instead of CS, and working as a hardware engineer for a decade before transitioning to software. He challenges the 'you either have it or you don't' narrative about programming talent, arguing that meta-skills like debugging persistence, bug tracking, learning how to approach hard problems, and working with mentors were more valuable than any specific technical knowledge. The post emphasizes that successful programming careers rarely follow the stereotypical 'whiz kid' path and that luck, serendipity, and supportive environments play major roles that are difficult to replicate as career advice.
key_points: Spent years failing to learn programming through BASIC, high school CS classes, and college courses before eventually succeeding
Worked as an electrical engineer at Centaur for 8 years, learning debugging, automation, and problem-solving from experienced mentors
Meta-skills (debugging persistence, bug tracking, approaching hard problems, communication) proved more valuable than specific technical knowledge
Most colleagues had similarly non-traditional paths, contradicting the 'talented whiz kid' stereotype
Career success involves so much luck and serendipity that extracting generalizable advice is nearly impossible
Reading broadly had mixed results - some books transformative (Design of Everyday Things), others wasteful (too many language books)
Functional programming advocacy and language fragmentation (Perl→Ruby→Python) were well-intentioned mistakes
money_quotes: If you believe in the common 'you either have it or you don't' narrative about programmers, I definitely don't have it. And yet, I now make a living programming, and people seem to be pretty happy with the work I do.

I find that I have a meta-skill set that I use all the time. There's nothing profound about the meta-skill set, but because I often work in new (to me) problem domains, I find my meta skillset to be more valuable than my actual skills.
The most common sort of career advice I see is 'you should do what I did because I'm successful'. It's usually phrased differently, but that's the gist of it. That basically never works.
Non-deterministic hardware bugs are so much worse than non-deterministic software bugs that I always believe I can track down software bugs.

It's just that writing seems undervalued in a way that makes it relatively easy to have outsized impact through writing. But the same forces that make it easy to have outsized impact also make it difficult to get paid well!
key_insight: The path to becoming a successful programmer is far more varied and luck-dependent than the industry's 'natural talent' narrative suggests, and the meta-skills of persistence, learning how to learn, and working with great mentors matter more than any specific technical knowledge or educational pedigree.
